target_audience.primary_persona: Software developers and AI engineers who want to automate coding tasks using multiple AI agents in parallel
secondary_personas: Engineering teams wanting to parallelize AI-assisted development workflows
DevOps engineers integrating AI coding agents into CI/CD pipelines
Solo developers managing complex multi-stage AI coding tasks
pain_points: Managing multiple AI agents (Claude, Codex, Aider, etc.) across different tasks is manual and error-prone
No reliable way to track state of long-running AI coding jobs
Rate limiting from a single provider stalls entire workflows
Hard to audit what an AI agent did, when, and with what output
No automated quality gate before AI-generated code is merged
goals: Run parallel AI coding jobs without manual supervision
Automatically failover between AI providers when rate-limited
Get clean audit logs and output artifacts from every job
Merge AI-produced code only when acceptance criteria pass
Keep the system simple, transparent, and crash-recoverable
target_audience.usage_context: Used locally on macOS (primary) or Linux by individual developers or small teams who want to delegate coding tasks to AI agents while they work on other things. Run as daemons via launchd/systemd or invoked ad-hoc from the CLI.

product_vision.one_liner: A filesystem-first orchestrator that runs parallel AI coding agents as first-class macOS document bundles, with automatic provider failover and merge-gated quality control.
product_vision.problem_statement: Developers using AI coding agents (Claude, Codex, Aider, etc.) must manually manage job state, handle rate limits, check outputs, and merge results—a fragile, manual process that doesn't scale to multiple concurrent agents.
product_vision.value_proposition: JobCard uses the filesystem itself as the state machine, making every job's status, history, and output visible in Finder, queryable via Spotlight, and recoverable after crashes with a simple `mv`. No database, no daemon memory, no opaque state—just files.
success_metrics: >95% job success rate across parallel agent runs
Zero data loss on crash—jobs return to pending via atomic rename
Developers can run 10+ concurrent AI jobs without manual intervention
<1s CLI response time for all status and control commands

existing_features: Core CLI (`jc`) with init, new, status, validate, dispatcher, merge-gate commands
Dispatcher loop with parallel job execution and provider failover
Merge gate with acceptance criteria validation and git integration
Adapter system for Claude, Codex, Goose, Aider, OpenCode, Ollama, and Mock
Job card template system with COW clones (APFS/Btrfs reflink)
macOS Quick Look plugin support and UTI registration
Launchd plist services for daemon management
Provider cooldown management (300s on rate-limit, exit code 75)
realtime module with feed validation types and unit tests
known_gaps: Missing CLI commands: retry, kill, logs, inspect, clean, providers, worktree
No global or project-level config file (~/.jobcard/config.yaml)
Model lookup system not yet implemented (designed in MODEL_LOOKUP.md)
realtime module not wired into dispatcher or CLI
No web UI or REST API
No multi-user or team collaboration features
No cross-job context sharing or persistent agent memory
technical_debt: All CLI commands implemented in a single main.rs (needs splitting into modules)
Two stub crates exist but are not implemented
No async processing—dispatcher uses synchronous file polling
No unit tests for jc CLI commands (only realtime module has tests)
Provider config format is minimal; no schema validation

alternatives: Aider (direct coding assistant, no orchestration layer)
LangChain / LangGraph (Python-based agent orchestration, higher complexity)
Vibe Kanban (pluggable executor, visual kanban focus)
GitHub Copilot Workspace (cloud-hosted, opaque state, single provider)
Devin / SWE-agent (autonomous coding agents, no local control plane)
Gas Town (multi-agent orchestration, inspiration for JobCard)
differentiators: Filesystem IS the state machine—no database, no opaque in-process state
macOS document bundles (UTI) with Finder/Spotlight/Quick Look integration
Zero-cost COW template cloning via APFS/Btrfs reflink
Automatic provider failover with cooldown across multiple AI backends
Crash recovery is a single `mv` command—no log replay needed
No GC languages in the critical path (Rust dispatcher, shell adapters)
Multi-stage jobs (spec→plan→implement→QA) with different agents per stage
competitive_context.market_position: A low-ceremony, crash-safe local orchestrator for developers who want to run multiple AI coding agents in parallel without adopting a heavy framework. Sits between simple CLI wrappers (Aider) and complex cloud platforms (Devin), targeting developers who value auditability, simplicity, and macOS-native integration.

technical: APFS COW clones require macOS 12+ (Linux falls back to Btrfs reflink or regular copy)
Quick Look plugin and UTI registration are macOS-only features
Adapter scripts are shell-based, limiting portability to POSIX systems
No database backend limits query/filter capabilities on large job sets
Filesystem polling has latency vs. event-based approaches (FSEvents/inotifywait)
resources: Appears to be a solo or very small team project (no git history, single main.rs pattern)
Early-stage with no established community or contributor base yet
Bootstrap-friendly business model implied—no external funding assumed
dependencies: AI provider APIs (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI Codex, Ollama local)
macOS 12+ for full feature set (APFS, Quick Look, launchd)
Optional: Ollama for local model support
